**LANES (Localised Alternative Navigation for Environs under Submersion): A Real-Time Flood-Adaptive Alternative Navigation System Utilizing NLP**

A Capstone Project presented to the

College of Computer Studies of  
Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Pasig

In partial fulfillment of the requirements for the degree of

Bachelor of Science in Information Technology

by:

Bellen, Jace H.

Cambe, Roi Yvann M.

Folloso, Chris Nicolai Z.

Noreen A. Perez, DIT

Adviser

Month, 2026

# **CHAPTER I** {#chapter-i}

**INTRODUCTION**

## **1.1 The Problem and its Background** {#1.1-the-problem-and-its-background}

Severe meteorological disruptions increasingly challenge urban mobility and commuter safety in coastal and tropical nations. In the geographic context of the Philippines, which experiences an average of 20 tropical cyclones annually, localized flooding has transitioned from a seasonal anomaly into a persistent logistical obstacle. During periods of torrential precipitation, metropolitan road networks frequently suffer structural or functional paralysis, stalling economic activities and trapping thousands of commuters in transit. Efficient transportation under these circumstances requires volatile, immediate structural data regarding roadway passability. However, the existing urban transit paradigm relies heavily on conventional navigation frameworks that assume static environmental conditions, leaving a distinct gap between active vehicle navigation and environmental risk mitigation.

Pasig City is one of the highly urbanized cities in Metro Manila that frequently experiences flooding during periods of intense rainfall and typhoons. Due to its proximity to the Pasig River, interconnected waterways, densely populated communities, and major transportation corridors, several roads become partially or completely impassable during heavy rainfall. Flooding not only disrupts the daily movement of commuters and motorists but also delays emergency response operations, public transportation, and the delivery of essential services. These recurring conditions highlight the need for a localized navigation solution that can provide timely and reliable flood-related route information within the city. Modern technology provides critical interventions through spatial computing, machine learning, and decentralized data collection to address disaster-induced transit failures. In the Philippines, historical frameworks have approached this issue primarily through macroscopic, predictive modeling or manual crowd-reporting. The University of the Philippines’ Nationwide Operational Assessment of Hazards (UP NOAH) delivers high-resolution hazard maps representing 5-year, 25-year, or 100-year rain return scenarios (Lagmay et al., 2024), while the Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration (PAGASA) supplies regional storm-surge models and real-time telemetric rain gauge metrics. Earlier local pioneering platforms like Project Agos (Rappler, 2014\) established the foundational viability of mapping digital crowdsourced crises for regional macro-monitoring and disaster dispatch.

While these platforms provide foundational data for long-term urban planning and disaster preparedness, recent local literature emphasizes that static systems lack the tactical immediacy required for active, real-time commuter routing. For instance, a study by Betchayda et al. (2025) on the FLONAV system demonstrated that integrating machine learning with automated routing algorithms can successfully calculate alternative paths around flooded vectors. However, physical infrastructure scaling remains constrained due to its reliance on localized Internet of Things (IoT) hardware and microcontroller deployment.

Furthermore, Research by Abana et al. (2019) verified that automated road-flood warning systems leveraging digital information dissemination achieve high accuracy in transmitting disaster alerts to the public. This finding aligns with global data from Alizadeh et al. (2022), which proved that augmenting routing networks with user-contributed, unstructured digital observations minimizes overall travel time and optimizes route safety by actively avoiding inundated streets.

Consequently, modern Natural Language Process (NLP) frameworks can bridge this infrastructure gap. By programmatically mining real-time disaster text, these frameworks bypass the financial and logistical constraints of hardware-centric sensor networks, transforming public text into structured spatial intelligence.

The operational framework of the LANES platform is situated within the Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) and Disaster Risk Management industries, specifically targeting the Digital Navigation and Mobility-as-a-Service (MaaS) sectors in the Philippines. Functioning as a Public Service Information Utility designed for the public good, the platform introduces a specialized layer focused on real-time hydrological hazards to establish a distinct niche within the urban mobility industry. Modern environmental platforms like LyfSaver PWA (LyfSaver PWA, 2024\) demonstrate the high utility of modern community-powered multi-hazard mobile dashboards for municipal hazard visibility; however, they function primarily as static informational hubs rather than active routing assets.

Road users in Pasig City experience several challenges whenever flooding occurs. First, existing navigation applications primarily monitor traffic congestion but do not accurately identify flooded roads, causing motorists to unknowingly pass through hazardous areas. Second, flood-related updates are often delayed and fragmented because information is scattered across local news websites, municipal announcements, social media posts, and community reports. As a result, commuters and motorists struggle to obtain reliable and timely information while traveling. Finally, although existing disaster monitoring platforms provide flood advisories, they do not automatically generate safe alternative routes based on real-time flood conditions, leaving users to manually determine their own travel paths. . This research is necessitated by the high fragmentation of real-time flood reports across digital channels. During severe weather events in municipalities like Pasig City, critical road path updates are obscured within thousands of informal, conversational "Taglish" posts (e.g., *"Baha na naman sa Caruncho Ave, lagpas tuhod"*), making manual compilation too slow for immediate navigation decisions. Despite the availability of flood monitoring platforms and navigation applications, a significant research gap remains between flood information dissemination and intelligent route generation. Existing systems generally present flood locations as static map markers or textual advisories but do not transform unstructured bilingual flood reports into routing constraints that can automatically guide commuters away from hazardous road segments. Moreover, many existing flood-adaptive navigation systems rely on expensive Internet of Things (IoT) sensors and specialized hardware, limiting their scalability and implementation in local government settings. This gap highlights the need for a software-driven solution capable of processing publicly available flood reports and converting them into real-time navigation assistance . To address these challenges, the proposed Localized Alternative Navigation for Environs under Submersion (LANES) utilizes Natural Language Processing (NLP) through a custom fine-tuned Named Entity Recognition (NER) model integrated with a rule-based keyword mapping dictionary. The system automatically extracts road names, landmarks, and flood-related information from bilingual English and Taglish reports collected from public news sources, municipal advisories, RSS feeds, and validated user submissions. These extracted entities are transformed into structured geospatial information, allowing the routing engine to generate flood-adaptive alternative routes that help commuters and motorists avoid hazardous road segments during flood events.

In developing this software-driven solution. The primary client of the proposed system is the Pasig City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (DRRMO), which plays a vital role in monitoring flood situations and disseminating disaster-related information to the public. Through coordination with the local DRRMO, the proposed system aims to support existing flood monitoring efforts by providing a platform that validates reported flood incidents and assists commuters in identifying safer alternative routes during flooding.

Ultimately, this study aims to demonstrate that combining Natural Language Processing (NLP) with open-source routing engines can automate the collection of disaster intelligence. This process transforms volatile social data into verified, actionable navigation commands, creating a reliable, proactive safety layer for the commuting public. 

## **1.2 Review of Related Literature** {#1.2-review-of-related-literature}

The increasing frequency and velocity of meteorological disasters in the Philippines have exposed deep operational limitations in traditional, isolated transit frameworks. To ground the development of an adaptive computational system, this review evaluates current global and local literature across five core interconnected domains: macro level hazard systems, community-based alert interfaces, flood-routing spatial networks, multi-modal crowdsourced mining, and mobile Progressive Web Application (PWA) engineering. By analyzing the structural intersections and operational gaps within these separate fields, this study establishes the technical and conceptual rationale for LANES a real time, flood-adaptive navigation platform that uses text-driven Natural Language Processing (NLP) to convert unstructured public reports into actionable spatial routing intelligence for urban commuters.

**Disaster Mapping and Early Warning Infrastructure**

In the Philippines, the established method to track disasters uses a centralized system that focuses on the safety of the public and the creation of models to predict warnings. For many years the University of the Philippines has managed the Nationwide Operational Assessment of Hazards (UP NOAH). It is a project that provides maps with a high level of detail to show where hazards are likely to occur during rain events that happen every 5, 25 and 100 years. As Lagmay et al. (2024) describe, the UP Resilience Institute changed this system when they added a tool to the live UP NOAH map that automatically predicts floods based on their effects. With this structure, the system connects large weather models with data collected from regional sensors over time. To help local areas, the system predicts when water will cover specific neighborhoods up to 24 hours before it happens.

But there is a difference in how long term city plans and tools for navigating vehicles are structured. In authoritative frameworks for hazards, the systems create maps that show risks for people to view. Those tools are not able to respond \-changing directions to move drivers away from streets that have water on them at that moment. There are systems of gauges for telemetry that the Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical besides Astronomical Services Administration (PAGASA) maintains those systems provide data about rainfall in the region that matches measured values closely. And yet the systems do not have the interfaces for network topology that are necessary if a person wants to change the plan for a path to a destination.

To bridge this operational gap, early localized web models turned toward public communications channels. Project Agos, launched by Rappler in 2014, pioneered the use of crowdsourced digital data via social media to plot disaster coordinates during typhoons. While highly successful as a static emergency incident map for resource dispatch and search and rescue teams, Project Agos lacked automated machine learning text pipelines to calculate depth severity, and it did not include a routing engine to calculate alternative paths for individual commuters.

Those constraints are also present in systems that track local history. In a broad review of how cities monitor floods, Song et al. (2024) organized modern methods into groups for hardware sensor networks and big data analytics. It is the conclusion of their study that future systems for resilience need to combine data from many sources. By doing this cities can reach high levels of precision that are available immediately. On that account the process does not require more money than city budgets allow for infrastructure. And although maps from the past and static forecasts show that users can see disaster data, those tools do not change the shapes of transit paths while events happen. Because of this missing function, LANES fills the gap. To solve the problem, the system changes text alerts into barriers for dynamic routing.

**Community-Based Disaster Information Platforms**

	Because centralized, sensor-driven government maps face high physical installation and maintenance costs, contemporary disaster response has shifted toward decentralized, citizen centric applications. In the local landscape, the LyfSaver PWA (2024) represents a modern, community powered multi hazard and climate tracking Progressive Web Application developed by Fyt Media, the UP Resilience Institute, and the YesPinoy Foundation. This system leverages localized, validated citizen reports to map real-time hazards like structural fires, localized landslides, and flash flooding at the street level.

Nevertheless, an operational gap remains between multi-hazard reporting dashboards and active, turn by turn navigation assets. While platforms like LyfSaver PWA provide high micro level visualization of localized urban threats, they function primarily as public informational hubs. They do not feature a network routing engine capable of calculating alternative transport lines or programmatically converting reported street coordinates into active vector barriers for routing engines. Information dashboards inform users that an obstacle exists, whereas a navigation application must recalculate a safe route around that obstacle.

Using citizen input to deal with a lack of data really gets results, according to international studies. See (2019) reviewed global citizen science and crowdsourcing projects for pluvial flooding, verifying that consumer mobile data provides high accuracy validation for spatial models across four core application types: automated street flooding detection via photos, real-time social media mining, localized web portals, and post event structural assessments.  Furthermore, Petersson et al. (2020) demonstrated that community mapped workflows offer an affordable, open source, and highly resilient alternative for urban flood modeling in resource-strained, data scarce municipal environments.

When those findings are combined, it is evident that data from citizens is sufficiently accurate for the creation of information about local spaces  but many current platforms only provide images for the public to view. LANES resolves this difference because it uses data from the community to do more than place markers on a map. It provides information to a spatial database; this database is what allows the routing engine to change as new information arrives.

**Flood Evacuation Routing and Spatial Network Modeling**

	Integrating dynamic hazard variables into path-generation logic requires structured routing network algorithms. A prominent local study focused on community level evacuation routing built a specialized spatial network for flood-prone areas surrounding the Lipadas River in Davao City (Manliguez et al., 2017). This research utilized high-resolution LiDAR-derived digital elevation models to compute safe escape pathways by combining a Capacity Aware Shortest Path Evacuation Routing (CASPER) algorithm with an Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP).

This research uses strong mathematical methods but it examines a large scale problem that involves emergency planning. In this situation, people who are at risk move along set city paths to reach public shelters that do not change during regional evacuations \- but people who travel in cities need a tool for navigation that works at an individual level. By using such a tool, a person is able to find paths that change as they move through different parts of city streets to reach personal locations.

More recently, local researchers have attempted to solve dynamic commuter rerouting by combining hardware with machine learning. The FLONAV system, developed by Betchayda et al. (2025), pairs ultrasonic physical sensors and ESP32 microcontrollers with predictive algorithms to handle real time flood tracking and dynamic navigation adjustments. Similarly, Abana et al. (2019) engineered an automated road flood warning architecture utilizing ultrasonic depth arrays to broadcast real-time roadway water levels via automated Twitter and Facebook streams, achieving a sensor precision rating of 98.69%.

Although systems like FLONAV and the automated road-flood warning system confirm that localized path changes are viable, their reliance on physical Internet of Things (IoT) hardware arrays presents substantial scaling and financial constraints in municipal settings. LANES bypasses these physical deployment limits by using software based NLP data extraction, matching the routing performance of sensor-driven systems at a significantly lower operational cost.

**Crowdsourced and Social Media Data for Flood Mapping**

	The technical viability of replacing physical hardware grids with digital public text relies on the maturity of social data mining models. Alizadeh et al. (2022) validated this methodology by introducing a human centered route optimization framework that paired physical flood gauges with crowdsourced street level media captured during Hurricane Harvey in Houston, Texas. Their study showed that adding user-shared digital info to official transit models really cuts travel time and vehicle wear by directing drivers around roadblocks with clear detours.

Using unstructured public observations for figuring out accurate water-depth data has improved in various media formats. Alizadeh and Behzadana's 2021 study shows how a deep neural network can process street photos to measure water depth. It does this by finding partially submerged stop signs and road parts, lowering depth estimation errors to just 12.63 inches.

While visual computer vision models require intensive processing power, text based data mining offers a faster, more lightweight alternative for real time applications. Bryan-Smith et al. (2023) They developed a real time text monitoring model using a transformer network to map flood severity layers through semantic text filtering and sentiment analytics of public text feeds. Their study shows that informal text can provide detailed geographic info to make precise hazard maps instantly. LANES uses this idea, designing its system to focus on text for quickly extracting depth data without the delays and data issues linked to image-based networks.

**Mobile Applications and Progressive Web Architectures**

Deploying an enterprise grade geospatial tool into the hands of commuters requires a lightweight, cross-platform software framework. Kangana et al. (2025) conducted a comprehensive review of 77 disaster management mobile applications on the Google Play Store, concluding that the most successful disaster readiness platforms combine artificial intelligence engines with crowdsourced reporting to maximize user engagement and system resilience.

In the Philippine urban context, this approach was demonstrated by the Mamamayan system built for Makati City. This platform featured real-time community emergency incident reporting and automated push notifications linked to a centralized administrative management panel. While effective for broad municipal emergency alerts, the Mamamayan system functioned primarily as a reporting and dispatch manager rather than an active routing tool.

The technical feasibility of deploying large, data-heavy geospatial mapping tools across mobile interfaces without requiring complex native app stores is validated by modern Progressive Web Application (PWA) architecture. Iadanza et al. (2021) developed the IdroGEO platform in Italy, a collaborative geospatial web mapping platform for tracking regional landslides and flood vectors. Utilizing PWA engineering, REST APIs, and open-source mapping libraries, the IdroGEO architecture delivered seamless, high-performance map processing across smartphones and desktops without requiring local installations.

Furthermore, because automated AI engines face risks of data hallucinations or incorrect extractions, the IdroGEO model verified the necessity of an administrative data-validation workflow. This design requirement is strongly supported by Bono et al. (2023), who developed a "human in the loop" framework for automated crisis informatics. Their architecture applies automatic machine learning classifiers to filter and geolocate social media updates, but queues the spatial outputs into an administrative dashboard for human validation before public broadcast. This dual stage validation model is directly integrated into the LANES system architecture to ensure high spatial reliability during critical weather events. 

Beyond system validation, the true urgency for this type of navigation safety layer is underscored by a localized study conducted by Tariverdi et al. (2023). Using Manila, Philippines, as an empirical case study, their research measured accessibility to public services and infrastructure criticality during disaster risk management events. They found an alarming stat: 22% of people in Manila lost access to crucial healthcare during heavy rain. When streets flooded just over 15 cm, a lot of folks couldn't get the help they needed. This shows why we urgently need LANES a flood-adaptive navigation system. It stops drivers from entering unsafe water, which is super important when roads fail. So, the city stays mobile, and people can reach vital services in crises.

**Natural Language Processing for Crisis Informatics**

	The core engine of the LANES platform relies on the automated transformation of unstructured text into structural database metrics, an area studied extensively within the field of crisis informatics. According to Imran et al. (2018), during mass emergencies, computational models for handling social messages focus on four key areas: filtering, sequence classification, entity extraction, and data aggregation  pretty useful for organizing info in crises. Their research established that the central challenges of crisis NLP involve parsing informal syntax, filtering high noise-to-signal ratios, and matching text extractions to physical map coordinates.

Despite these textual challenges, automated systems can reliably derive spatial data from unstructured messaging. Singh et al. (2017) built a machine learning framework that classified flood related text feeds into priority levels and mapped affected users using a Markov chain location prediction matrix, achieving a classification accuracy of 81% and a spatial location mapping accuracy of 87%.

Furthermore, Dwarakanath et al. (2021) conducted a systematic review of machine learning applications for emergency response via social networks, highlighting that while substantial literature exists on the classification of disaster text, a significant gap remains regarding platforms that connect text classification directly to an active routing network engine. Most systems map locations as text for dispatch logs rather than processing them as mathematical routing barriers.

The technical gap matters a lot for how people talk in the Philippines. During typhoons, locals share updates using a mix of Filipino and English, like *"Baha na naman sa may Caruncho, lagpas tuhod na,"* which means the streets are waist deep water and sedans can't pass. Now, global systems work best with structured English data. But Named Entity Recognition tools aren't picky about languages. The LANES setup tweaks those existing tools to deal with Filipino English mixes and translate local slang measurements into useful flood maps.

### **Comparison of Natural Language Processing Algorithms for Flood Information Extraction**

Selecting an appropriate Natural Language Processing (NLP) algorithm is a crucial step in developing a system capable of accurately extracting flood-related information from unstructured bilingual reports. Since the proposed LANES system aims to identify road names, landmarks, municipalities, and flood-related descriptions from online news articles and community-generated reports, several Named Entity Recognition (NER) approaches were reviewed to determine the most appropriate algorithm for the study.

One of the earliest and widely adopted sequence-labeling algorithms is **Conditional Random Fields (CRF)**. CRF is a probabilistic machine learning algorithm that predicts entity labels by considering the contextual relationship between neighboring words within a sentence. It has demonstrated reliable performance in structured text and formal documents due to its ability to model sequential dependencies. However, CRF relies heavily on manually engineered features and becomes less effective when processing noisy, informal, or multilingual text commonly found in social media posts and public flood reports. These limitations reduce its adaptability to bilingual English–Filipino disaster-related content.

An improvement over traditional CRF is the **Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory with Conditional Random Fields (BiLSTM-CRF)** model. This hybrid approach combines the contextual learning capability of Bidirectional LSTM neural networks with the sequence-labeling strength of CRF. Unlike CRF, BiLSTM-CRF automatically learns textual features from training data, allowing it to better recognize contextual relationships within a sentence. As a result, it generally achieves higher entity recognition accuracy than traditional statistical methods. Nevertheless, BiLSTM-CRF requires larger annotated datasets, longer training time, and greater computational resources, making implementation more demanding for localized disaster management systems.

Recent advancements in NLP have introduced **Transformer-based Named Entity Recognition (NER)** models, which utilize contextual embeddings to understand semantic relationships among words regardless of their position in a sentence. According to the comparative study conducted by Lothritz et al. (2020), transformer-based models such as BERT, RoBERTa, and XLNet consistently outperformed CRF and BiLSTM-CNN-CRF across multiple fine-grained NER datasets in terms of F1-score, demonstrating stronger contextual understanding and better generalization across different domains. These capabilities make transformer-based NER particularly suitable for extracting location entities and disaster-related information from multilingual and unstructured textual reports.

Considering the characteristics of the proposed LANES system, **Fine-Tuned Transformer-based Named Entity Recognition** was selected as the primary information extraction algorithm. Unlike traditional CRF and BiLSTM-CRF models, transformer-based NER effectively recognizes contextual relationships, informal expressions, abbreviations, and bilingual English–Filipino or Taglish reports frequently used by commuters during flood events. These capabilities enable the proposed system to accurately identify road names, municipalities, landmarks, and flood severity descriptions from unstructured textual data before converting them into structured geospatial information for adaptive route generation.

Table  presents the comparison of the reviewed algorithms. 

| Algorithm | Primary Application | Strengths | Limitations | Suitability for LANES |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Conditional Random Fields (CRF)** | Sequence labeling and Named Entity Recognition | High performance on structured text; interpretable results | Requires manual feature engineering; less effective on informal multilingual text | Moderate |
| **Bidirectional LSTM with CRF (BiLSTM-CRF)** | Context-aware Named Entity Recognition | Learns contextual features automatically; higher recognition accuracy than CRF | Requires large annotated datasets and higher computational resources | High |
| **Fine-Tuned Transformer-based Named Entity Recognition (Selected)** | Named Entity Recognition for multilingual and unstructured text | Strong contextual understanding, multilingual capability, adaptable through transfer learning, high extraction accuracy | Requires pre-trained models and fine-tuning | **Very High (Selected)** |

	The proposed LANES system will utilize a fine-tuned transformer-based Named Entity Recognition (NER) model trained using a curated dataset composed of bilingual (English and Filipino) flood-related reports relevant to Pasig City, collected from publicly available online news articles, government advisories, and validated community-generated reports. Only data pertaining to locations, incidents, and flood conditions within Pasig City will be included in the dataset to ensure the model's relevance and accuracy for the target area. The dataset will contain annotated entities such as road names, landmarks, barangays, flood depth descriptions, and flood severity indicators specific to Pasig City. These annotated entities will enable the model to extract location-specific flood information and transform unstructured textual reports into structured geospatial data that can be used to generate flood-adaptive alternative routes for commuters and motorists within Pasig City. 

What particular ML or AI algorithm are you going to used in the study?

Compare atleast 3 Algorithms related to your study then, select the best

How about the dataset? Describe the dataset. The data to be trained

### **Synthesis** {#synthesis}

The review literature indicates a growing ecosystem of disaster response technologies within the Philippines, ranging from macro scale scientific forecasting models like UP NOAH (Lagmay et al., 2024\) to fixed community evacuation routing frameworks (Manligues et al., 2017\) and live public dashboards like LyfSaver and Project Agos. Nevertheless, a consistent operational gap emerges across these platforms: current systems are designed either for long term predictive planning, static crisis mapping, administrative resource dispatch, or emergency evacuation along pre-determined shelter pathways. None of these systems provides real-time, depth aware, turn by turn alternative route navigation for independent urban commuters trying to travel to arbitrary personal locations.

Global research in crisis informatics, transformer based sentiment analysis, and street level depth processing confirms the technical readiness of deriving actionable hazard data from unstructured text feeds. Local alternative systems like FLONAV and the automated road flood warning system explore dynamic path alterations but rely on physical IoT hardware grids that limit wide geographic scalability.

LANES addresses these overlapping gaps by bridging three domains that existing research treats separately:

1. Automated NLP- driven extraction of fine-grained flood locations and depth metrics from unstructured local multilingual text data.  
2. The automatic transformation of these text extractions into spatial avoidance buffers within a specialized geographic database.  
3. The dynamic recalculation of alternate route geometries through an open-source routing engine that treats highly flooded streets as impassable physical walls.

	By integrating these features into a single, lightweight, cross platform Progressive Web Application, LANES updates passive disaster warning maps into an active, commuter centric driving helper designed for the infrastructure realities of Philippine cities.

## 

## **1.3 Significance of the Study** {#1.3-significance-of-the-study}

The development and implementation of the LANES (Localised Alternative Navigation for Environs under Submersion) platform provides direct, measurable contributions across multiple institutional, public, and academic tiers. Guided by the project’s primary objectives, core scope, and technical features, the outcomes of this research will benefit the following entities:

**The City of Pasig and Local Disasters Risk Reduction Management (DRRM) Units**

	This study's results will help local disaster responders in Pasig too. It gives them an automated real-time safety net for their streets. Plus, the Validation Dashboard offers a helpful tool. It lets City officials and DRRM units track and monitor flood risks efficiently, and even audit them when needed. Rather than relying on scattered community phone calls or manual message sorting, this system translates colloquial text data into validated map layers, streamlining municipal resource deployment. The visual interface mapping real-time hydrological hazards allows emergency dispatchers to view precisely where logistics lines are compromised. This directive improves route safety for emergency supply deliveries and optimizes dispatch paths for search and rescue teams during active typhoon events.

**Commuters and Motorists**

	This research provides immediate urban mobility solutions and safety enhancements for everyday road users navigating localized metropolitan hubs. Through the platform’s cross platform Progressive Web Application (PWA) interface, users are equipped with a Flood Adaptive Route Calculation service that actively avoids impassable coordinates. This system shields both computers and drivers by rerouting them around risky flood areas. It does this by offering real-time turn-by-turn directions. The cool thing is its Severity Visualization feature which turns Tagalog-English depth phrases like *"hanggang-tuhod*" or *"lagpas-dibdib"* into easy to read colors. Users then use these visuals to choose safer routes. This helps in avoiding engine damage from floods, eases traffic, and keeps walkers safe from dangerous currents.

**PLP and IT/CS Student Community**

As an academic contribution to the Information Technology Program of Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Pasig, this study serves as a functional, high-impact framework for localized application development. It provides a real-world demonstration that advanced Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and Named Entity Recognition (NER) models can be efficiently paired to solve community infrastructure problems.This project shows that we can create super useful disaster tools with open source libraries. They cost less since we skip pricey IoT systems, making our local software studies richer.

**Researchers**

	This study pushes researchers to grow in both tech skills and management. On a technical level, the research deepens execution knowledge in text tokenization, bilingual Natural Language Processing pipelines, spatial database querying using PostGIS, and dynamic geospatial route generation via open-source engines. From a project management and managerial perspective, it refines the ability to analyze local market needs, categorize software within the urban mobility industry, design administrative approval workflows, and translate complex technical architecture into targeted public service utilities.

**Future Researchers**

	This research sets up a strong foundation for future developers aiming to build advanced, data-driven navigation systems. It gives them an excellent reference model to scale real time crisis informatics systems too. Moreover, the system could be retooled to include more sophisticated algorithms, like integrating deep neural networks for extracting depth information from user-submitted road photos. Plus, it could process real-time data from social media APIs. The study also serves as a dependable guide for moving from passive hazard mapping to more interactive, automated obstacle avoidance systems.

## **1.4 Statement of the Problem** {#1.4-statement-of-the-problem}

Flooding continues to disrupt transportation and daily mobility in Pasig City by making roads inaccessible and limiting the availability of reliable route information. Although existing navigation and disaster monitoring systems provide useful information, they do not automatically extract flood-related reports from multiple sources and generate localized alternative routes for commuters. To address this gap, the proposed Localized Alternative Navigation for Environs under Submersion (LANES) aims to utilize Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Named Entity Recognition (NER) to process flood-related reports and provide flood-adaptive route recommendations. Specifically, this study seeks to answer the following questions:

1. How can Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Named Entity Recognition (NER) be used to extract flood-related information from bilingual reports, social media posts, and news articles and convert them into structured location data?  
2. How can the system classify flood severity and visualize road conditions using standardized map indicators to help users easily identify safe and flooded routes?  
3. What software architecture and algorithmic strategies can be implemented to dynamically convert verified flood coordinates into impassable geometric barriers within an open-source routing network engine to automatically render alternative, clear detour paths?  
4. How can the proposed system assist the Pasig City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office (DRRMO) in validating flood reports before providing route recommendations to users?

## **1.5 Scope and Limitation of the Study** {#1.5-scope-and-limitation-of-the-study}

	The development of the LANES platform focuses on establishing a software-driven, real time navigation safety layer for urban commuters. To ensure a realistic and manageable development cycle, the technical features and operational boundaries of the project are defined as follows:

### **Scope** {#scope}

The development of the Progressive Web Application will include:

1. **Natural Language Processing (NLP) Extraction:** Programmatically mining unstructured textual descriptions to isolate spatial markers and hazard metrics. This is specifically focused on Named Entity Recognition (NER) to identify street names and detect bilingual "Taglish" flood depth phrases in real time.  
2. **Severity Visualization and Mapping:** translating height benchmarks into structured, color-coded geospatial coordinates to map live road conditions. Inundation data is categorized into four standard visual tiers based on public safety thresholds:  
   1. **White (Low / Passable):** Ankle-deep water (10cm to 20cm) that presents no immediate threat to any profile.  
   2. **Yellow (Moderate / Hazardous to Sedans):** Knee-deep water (21cm to 50cm), which presents clear structural risks for low-clearance vehicles and pedestrians.  
   3. **Orange (High / Impassable to Light Vehicles):** Waist-to-chest-deep water (51cm to 140cm) that renders road segments dangerous for standard cars and motorcycles.  
   4. **Red (Extreme/Completely Impassable):** Neck-deep water or higher (above 140cm), indicating severe structural submersion.  
3. **Flood-Adaptive Route Calculation:** Computing real-time transit directions through an open-source network routing engine. The system treats any database coordinate marked with Orange or Red severity levels as a physical road barrier, automatically rendering alternative safe detour paths for the user.  
4. **Queue-Based Content Management:** Operating a dedicated administrative dashboard for municipal data validation. This system queues all raw AI text extractions into a secure moderation feed, allowing authorized local disaster units to review, approve, or discard flood events before they are broadcast to the public interface.  
5. **Cross-Platform Delivery:** Deploying the entire mapping engine using Progressive Web Application (PWA) architecture. This strategy guarantees responsive, mobile-first accessibility across web browsers and smartphones without requiring traditional native application store installation.  
6. **Dashboard:** Providing administrators with a real-time overview of system activity, including the number of pending reports, active detours, daily approvals, total registered users, and overall system health status.  
7. **Monitoring Module:** Allowing administrators to monitor live flood zones and active detours through an administrative map view, along with continuous monitoring of the spatial database, authentication service, and routing service to ensure system stability.  
8. **Report Generation:** Providing administrators with tools to generate and export system records for documentation and analysis purposes. This includes:  
   1. **Flood Report Records** – detailing submitted, approved, and rejected flood reports along with their severity classification.  
   2. **User Activity Records** – summarizing registered users and their account status.  
   3. **Data Export Reports** – allowing administrative data to be exported in CSV and JSON formats for external record-keeping or further analysis.  
9. **User Account and Access Management:** Supporting user registration, login, and secure authentication through JWT, along with Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) to define permissions for Super Admin, DRRM Officer, Moderator, and Commuter roles.  
10. **Real-Time Updates:** Synchronizing flood reports, detours, and map changes instantly across the platform using WebSocket integration.  
11. **Offline Capability:** Supporting basic offline functionality through Progressive Web App (PWA) features, including cached maps and offline warning notifications during connectivity loss.

### **Limitations** {#limitations}

The system will, however, not include or support the following features:

1. **Hardware and Physical IoT Sensor Integration:** The project operates exclusively as a software-based utility. It will not deploy, maintain, or connect to physical ultrasonic water-level sensors, microcontrollers, or external physical telemetry hardware arrays.  
2. **Direct Private Social Media API Integration:** Due to strict developer access limitations, paywalls, and privacy restrictions on major platforms, the system will not pull real-time data directly from private enterprise social media database streams. Real-time parsing will instead rely on accessible text inputs, including RSS feeds, local news articles, and simulated mock public data feeds.  
3. **Native Autonomous Control and Voice Navigation:** The software provides strictly screen-based visual maps and turn-by-turn route calculations. It will not provide voice-guided audio assistance, nor will it interface with any native vehicle hardware systems to mechanically alter or operate a car.  
4. **Multimodal Data Analytics and Image Processing:** Computer vision algorithms, deep learning neural networks for photo analysis, and generalized social media sentiment analysis have been considered but are not included in this system. The platform's processing engine is strictly limited to extracting geospatial entities from text strings.  
5. **Geographic Coverage Constraints:** To ensure precise evaluation during the capstone defense phase, the spatial routing network and live data simulations will be restricted to a specific localized metropolitan sector within the City of Pasig rather than mapping the entire national transit grid.

# 

# **CHAPTER II** {#chapter-ii}

**METHODOLOGY**

## **2.1 Introduction** {#2.1-introduction}

	This chapter details the methodological framework used to design, build, and evaluate the LANES (Localised Alternative Navigation for Environs under Submersion) platform. This section acts as the technical blueprint for the study, clearly explaining how the research was done. It changes the project's conceptual goals into a solid, real world software engineering plan. The chapter lays out the research design, system structure, data pipelines, and user tests. Showing that the platform is not only technically sound but also super reliable, it highlights all the methodical steps taken.

	The selected methodology is specifically structured to balance technical development rigor with real world utility in crisis environments. It provides a transparent, reproducible account of how raw, bilingual text data is programmatically captured, validated, and transformed into a life-saving navigation asset tailored for Filipino commuters and local disaster risk management units.

## **2.2 Research Design** {#2.2-research-design}

	This study uses a Developmental Research Design with a quantitative approach. This design was chosen because the main output of this study is not just data or analysis, but an actual working system, the LANES platform, that needs to be built, tested, and evaluated. The research process moves through five general stages: analysis of the problem, design of the system, development of the software, implementation in a pilot area, and evaluation of its performance.

The development stage follows the Agile methodology explained in Section 2.2.3, where the system is built through repeated cycles or sprints. This allows the research team to test and improve each part of the system, such as the NLP model, the spatial database, and the routing engine, before moving to the next stage.

The quantitative part of this study is used mainly to evaluate the finished system. This is done through user assessment questionnaires based on the ISO/IEC 25010 Software Product Quality Model, which is a standard used to measure software quality. This study focuses on four main quality characteristics from this model:

* **Functional Suitability** – how accurate the system is in extracting flood information and generating correct alternative routes.  
* **Performance Efficiency** – how fast the system responds and recalculates a new route.  
* **Usability** – how easy and clear the system is to use, measured using the System Usability Scale (SUS).  
* **Reliability** – how consistent the system performs, especially in the flood report validation process.

Respondents, including commuters, DRRM personnel, and IT experts, will answer questionnaires using a Likert scale for each of these areas. The results will be analyzed using basic statistics such as mean, standard deviation, and percentage, along with the precision, recall, and F1-score results from the NLP testing in Section 2.2.2. This combination of system development and ISO/IEC 25010-based evaluation will show whether LANES is not only technically working, but also usable and reliable for its intended users.

### **2.2.1 Architectural Framework** {#2.2.1-architectural-framework}

	The LANES platform uses a software driven, three-tier architecture to transform conversational public text reports into live, flood-adaptive navigation routes. By relying entirely on code and data streams, this setup completely eliminates the need for expensive physical water sensors or hardware installations. To process information smoothly, the system divides its operations into five simple layers: the Data Ingestion Layer, the Linguistic Processing Engine, the Spatial Persistence Layer, the Routing Topology Interface, and the Cross-Platform Presentation Layer. 

**![][image1]**

*Figure 1\. The LANES Horizontal 3-Tier Software Architecture and System Data Flow Framework*

	The Presentation Tier acts as the bridge between end-users and system admins, separating client-side rendering from core app logic. In this layer, the PWA Commuter Client is a cross-platform Progressive Web App. It handles routing questions and adds real-time detour routes right onto what users see. At the same time, the DRRM Admin Dashboard lets Pasig City disaster responders securely check updates. They can confirm, adjust, or scrap flood info before it gets added to the database.Both these interfaces use the Mapbox GL Geospatial Canvas. This tool pulls live flood layer data to show risk zones in coded colors and suggest safe routes based on updated paths. Usually, a commuter would ask for a trip plan on their phone, while a DRRM worker might be reviewing an uncertain flood warning nearby the Pasig Mega Market at the same time.

	The Application Logic Tier acts as the computational brain of the platform, coordinating background automation, natural language parsing, and graph-network routing optimizations via a high-performance Tauri and Rust backend. This tier utilizes Automated Stream Scrapers lightweight background service workers that continuously poll open public data channels like RSS feeds, municipal notices, and citizen reports to output raw, unstructured text strings. These strings are instantly ingested by the Bilingual NLP Parser, which tokenizes the text and runs a custom Named Entity Recognition model fine-tuned to extract street-level location entities and translate colloquial Taglish depth markers into structured hazard metadata. Once this metadata is processed, the Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM) updates its localized map topology graph of Pasig City, injecting active avoidance polygons into its cost matrix and setting the edge weights of affected roads to infinity (∞) to calculate a dynamic alternate path around submersed vectors.

	The Data Persistence Tier provides secure data storage, spatial indexing, and geometric buffering to convert linguistic variables into actionable geographic blockades. At the core of this tier sits the PostgreSQL and PostGIS Spatial Database, an enterprise-grade relational ledger that houses baseline urban road network datasets and maintains the active record of validated flood reports. Working alongside the database, the OSM Geocoder and Polygon Generator translates plain-text street tokens into precise OpenStreetMap coordinates. Once these coordinates are approved via the DRRM administrative confirmation step, the module uses PostGIS spatial functions to automatically expand the single coordinate point into a 50-meter geometric avoidance polygon covering the physical span of the flooded road segment. Through this pipeline, an informal string like Caruncho Avenue is geocoded, transformed into a spatial block upon administrative approval, and piped back to the logic and presentation tiers to trigger real-time route deflections.

### **2.2.2 Algorithm**  {#2.2.2-algorithm}

	The LANES platform works through a series of data transformations that turn raw text reports into flood-adaptive navigation routes. Figure 2 shows the Level 1 Data Flow Diagram, which illustrates how the system processes information from data collection to route generation, including the point where human validation takes place. The system's execution flow is divided into three main phases: bilingual text extraction, spatial data transformation, and route recalculation. 

**Bilingual Named Entity Recognition (NER) and Severity Mapping**

	The system will use a fine-tuned Transformer-based Named Entity Recognition (NER) model as the main machine learning component. This model was chosen because it performs better than older methods like CRF or BiLSTM-CRF, especially when handling informal and bilingual text such as Taglish flood reports. The model works together with a rule-based keyword dictionary to fully process each report.

* The **NER Model** identifies and extracts street names, landmarks, and location-related words from unstructured text, while ignoring unrelated words or casual chatter.  
* The **Rule-Based Dictionary** scans the text for common depth-related words (such as "baha" or "tuhod") and matches them to a specific flood severity level.

The model will be trained using unstructured, bilingual (English and Taglish) text related to flooding. This includes news headlines, social media style posts, RSS feeds, municipal advisories, and citizen-submitted reports. The main features and variables used in training are:

* **Location Entities:** street names, intersections, and landmarks (e.g., Caruncho Ave)  
* **Severity Keywords:** depth-related expressions (e.g., "hanggang tuhod," "lagpas dibdib")  
* **Target Labels:** four severity categories based on water depth (White, Yellow, Orange, Red)

The dataset will come from a mix of public sources and manually prepared samples:

1. **Public Data Sources:** RSS feeds, local news articles, and official Pasig City advisories.  
2. **Curated Training Dataset:** A manually annotated dataset of 300 to 500 flood-related text samples in English and Taglish, created by the research team. Each sample will be labeled with the correct location and severity level to train and test the model.  
3. **User Simulation Data:** Additional text inputs collected from commuters and motorists during the system's testing phase, used to further evaluate the model in a real usage setting.

To achieve reliable training results, the study will use **300 to 500 annotated text samples** as the target dataset size. This number was chosen because the model needs to recognize multiple entity types (location names, landmarks, and severity expressions) across two languages. A smaller dataset, such as 50 to 100 samples, would not provide enough variation for the model to learn patterns accurately and would likely result in low precision and recall scores. The dataset will be divided using a 70-15-15 split, with 70% used for training, 15% for validation, and 15% for testing.

**Step-by-Step Process of the Algorithm**

1. **Data Collection.** The system collects raw text from public sources such as RSS feeds, news articles, and citizen reports.  
2. **Text Cleaning and Tokenization.** The raw text is cleaned and broken down into individual words or tokens to prepare it for analysis.  
3. **Entity Recognition.** The NER model scans the tokens and identifies location-related words, such as street names and landmarks.  
4. **Severity Detection.** At the same time, the rule-based dictionary checks the tokens for depth-related expressions and matches them to a severity level.  
5. **Model Evaluation.** The system compares the extracted results against the labeled dataset to compute precision, recall, and F1-score, confirming how accurate the extraction process is.

Once the model receives raw text, it first cleans and breaks the text down into individual words through a process called tokenization. These words are then analyzed by the NER model to identify street names and landmarks, while the rule-based dictionary checks for depth-related expressions and matches them to a severity level.

To measure how accurate this extraction process is, the system uses three common evaluation metrics: precision, recall, and F1-score. These formulas check how many of the extracted results are correct and how many actual flood reports were successfully detected.

Precision \= True Positives (TP)True Positives (TP) \+ False Positives (FP)

Recall \= True Positives (TP)True Positives (TP) \+ False negatives (FN)

F1-Score \=2  True Positives (TP)True Positives (TP) \+ False Positives (FP)

* Sample Data Transformation: The scraper captures a raw citizen text submission stating: *“Baha sa Caruncho Ave Pasig malapit sa mega market lagpas tuhod na raw.”* The tokenization and custom NER pipeline isolate the string token Caruncho Ave as a location entity (TP), while filtering out conversational noise. The keyword scanner flags the modifier “*lagpas tuhod”* and maps it directly to the structured Orange/High Severity inundation metadata tier.

**Spatial Geocoding and PostGIS Geometric Buffering**

	Once the plain-text location entity and severity tier are extracted, they are packaged into a structured payload and transmitted across the application layer to the OSM Geocoder. This module cross-references the text token with the OpenStreetMap geographical database to extract an exact latitude and longitude coordinate point. Because a single coordinate point cannot accurately represent the real-world physical span of a flooded road, the geocoded point must be transformed into a geometric area. Before this spatial transformation occurs, the report is held in a mutable state within the pending queue. Once a Pasig City DRRM officer executes an administrative approval action, the coordinate point is committed to the PostgreSQL database, and a PostGIS spatial buffering query is instantly executed. The spatial engine calculates a 50-meter geometric expansion around the coordinate point using a standardized spatial reference identifier (SRID 4326\) to generate a bounding polygon that covers the surrounding street segments.

* Sample Data Transformation: The extracted text token Caruncho Ave is geocoded by the infrastructure stack into the coordinate pair point \[14.559152964563808, 121.08083648286963\]. Upon receiving manual DRRM administrative confirmation, the system runs an automated spatial buffering function:

  ST\_Buffer(ST\_SetSRID(ST\_MakePoint(14.559152964563808, 121.08083648286963), 4326)::geography, 50)::geometry

**Graph Network Routing and Cost Matrix Optimization**

The final phase executes when a commuter submits a navigation query containing an origin point A and a destination point B. The Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM) processes this request using a localized road network graph topology of Pasig City, where intersections are treated as nodes (V) and interconnecting street segments are treated as edges (E). Under normal driving conditions, the routing engine utilizes standard edge weights (W), where the cost of traveling across a street segment is determined by its physical distance (d) and legal speed limit (s).

To force real-time route deflections around flooded areas, the system intercepts the routing loop. OSRM runs a spatial intersection query between the commuter's baseline path and all active avoidance polygons stored in the PostGIS database. If a road edge intersects an active flood polygon, the system modifies the cost matrix by dynamically updating the edge weight of that specific road segment to infinity. The modified cost function for any given edge (e) in the network graph is expressed mathematically as follows:

W(e)={ ∞, if ePflood ⊘ ds, if ePflood \=⊘  }

	Where d is is the edge distance, $s$ is the baseline speed limit, $e$ is the active road segment vector, and Pflood represents the set of all validated, 50-meter administrative flood avoidance polygons. By setting intersecting edge costs to infinity, the routing engine's pathfinding algorithm treats the flooded street as an impassable barrier, forcing the system to compute an optimized, alternative detour coordinates array around the hazard. 

Sample Data Transformation: A commuter requests a route passing through the city center. OSRM detects that the baseline route intersects the 50-meter avoidance polygon generated for Caruncho Ave. The system updates the cost matrix, changing the weight of the Caruncho Avenue edge from its standard travel time cost to ∞. The pathfinding engine recalculates the route, deflecting the polyline array onto adjacent, dry streets, and pipes the safe detour coordinates back to the Mapbox GL canvas on the commuter's Progressive Web Application.

### **2.2.3 SE Paradigm** {#2.2.3-se-paradigm}

	To effectively manage the development, algorithmic testing, and system integration of the platform, the project is executed using the Agile Software Development Methodology. Because the system relies heavily on interlocking data-driven modules—specifically the natural language parsing pipeline, the PostGIS geometric buffer engine, and the OSRM routing cost matrix—a rigid, sequential development model is impractical. The Agile framework allows the development lifecycle to be broken down into highly adaptive, repeating cycles known as sprints. This iterative approach ensures that the linguistic accuracy of the parsing model can be measured, evaluated, and refined continuously before deploying the dependent spatial and routing features.

 ![][image2]

*Figure 3\. The Agile Iterative Software Development Lifecycle Framework for the LANES Platform*

**1\. Requirements Planning and Analysis**

In this phase, the core functional and non-functional requirements of the application are defined based on the operational needs of Pasig City commuters and municipal DRRM officers. The scope of data ingestion streams, the linguistic variations of Taglish flood reports, and the performance thresholds for dynamic routing calculations are mapped out. These requirements are broken down into discrete technical tasks and compiled into a product backlog to establish the baseline parameters for individual development sprints.

**2\. System and Architecture Design**

During this phase, the logical boundaries and structural interactions of the application's components are designed. The conceptual workflows established in the Level 1 Data Flow Diagram are translated into concrete technical blueprints, including horizontal 3-tier architecture layouts and relational database schemas. This ensures that data interfaces, API endpoints, and communication channels between the Tauri-Rust core engine, the PostgreSQL/PostGIS storage layer, and the client-side Mapbox GL frontend canvas are systematically organized before any code is generated.

**3\. Iterative Development (Sprint Execution)**

The development phase is broken down into continuous, focused sprints where individual software components are programmed and incrementally integrated. Rather than attempting to build the entire system at once, modules are developed in a deliberate, logical sequence based on data dependencies. The background stream scrapers and the bilingual NLP parsing models are constructed first. Once the system can successfully isolate location and severity tokens from unstructured text strings, subsequent sprints focus on building the spatial geocoding engine, the PostGIS polygon generation queries, and finally, the OSRM dynamic cost matrix routing adjustments.

**4\. Quality Assurance and Algorithmic Testing**

As each incremental module is completed, it undergoes rigorous quality assurance to verify structural integrity and algorithmic precision. The bilingual NLP parser is tested against curated verification datasets to mathematically calculate its precision, recall, and F1\- Score metrics. Simultaneously, the spatial buffering routines are verified to ensure they generate geometrically valid polygons, while the pathfinding algorithm is tested with simulated blockades to confirm that the generated detour arrays successfully avoid flooded street coordinates. Any identified processing errors or logic gaps are logged and immediately funneled into the next sprint for resolution.

**5\. Deployment and Review**

At the conclusion of a development cycle, the verified software increments are compiled into an operational build for review and evaluation. The Progressive Web Application client and the administrative dashboard interfaces are deployed to a localized staging environment. This deployment allows for an end-to-end evaluation of the data transformation pipeline—tracing a raw text string through geocoding, administrative verification, and path recalculation—ensuring that the system functions cohesively and meets all defined capstone requirements before final production release.

	In conclusion, the Agile Software Development Methodology provides a structured, adaptive operational framework that directly mitigates the technical risks inherent in the LANES platform. By dividing the software architecture into independent sprint cycles, the development process shifts away from rigid linear development to focus on systematic module verification. This ensures that the foundational bilingual natural language processing pipeline is thoroughly tested, refined, and mathematically optimized for high precision and recall before initiating dependent spatial buffering and graph network routing workloads. Ultimately, this paradigm establishes a continuous feedback loop between iterative programming and rigorous algorithmic validation, guaranteeing that the integrated system functions as a cohesive, reliable, and entirely software-driven alternative to physical hardware infrastructure.

### **2.2.4 Tool to test for Accuracy** {#2.2.4-tool-to-test-for-accuracy}

	To evaluate the precision and behavioral integrity of the core system algorithms, the development architecture integrates specialized automated testing utilities and validation frameworks. Because the platform relies heavily on natural language processing to extract data, the machine learning components are subjected to isolated testing phases using Python-based machine learning assessment frameworks alongside the core Rust backend testing environment.

The custom, fine-tuned Bilingual Named Entity Recognition (NER) sequence-labeling model is thoroughly evaluated using the scikit-learn and seqeval testing libraries in an isolated verification environment. During the quality assurance testing phase, a holdout validation dataset consisting of annotated, colloquial Taglish flood reports is processed through the extraction pipeline. The testing utility automatically compares the predicted token classifications against the ground-truth annotations to capture performance metrics. This validation script explicitly measures precision, recall, and  F1\- Score arrays across individual entity classes, ensuring that localized geographic identifiers, landmark tokens, and conversational depth modifiers are extracted with high semantic accuracy. These automated validation procedures ensure that any regression in linguistic accuracy or degradation in model confidence is instantly flagged and logged before a code iteration is approved for staging.

Beyond the machine learning classification layers, the spatial and routing engines are systematically validated using integrated unit and integration testing workflows built directly into the Tauri and Rust application layer. The PostGIS geometric expansion routines are verified using automated database test suites that assert the spatial accuracy, vertex count, and proper Coordinate Reference System alignment of the generated 50-meter bounding polygons. Simultaneously, the Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM) integration is validated using simulated routing injection scripts. These scripts programmatically transmit origin and destination coordinate pairs that cross known, simulated flood zones. The testing tool then intercepts the output payload to programmatically assert that the generated detour polyline array does not intersect the active avoidance polygon vectors. This end-to-end algorithmic testing framework guarantees that both the machine learning predictions and downstream spatial calculations remain completely accurate and reliable throughout continuous development cycles.

## **2.3 Population and Sampling** {#2.3-population-and-sampling}

The study focuses on individuals who are the primary stakeholders in flood-affected urban mobility within Pasig City, where the development and pilot testing of the LANES system is being conducted. These are:

**Commuters and Motorists.** Twenty-five (25) to fifty (50) individuals who regularly commute or drive within the designated pilot testing area (e.g., the surrounding streets of the Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Pasig campus or a specific city sector in Pasig). These participants will directly interact with the LANES application to test route calculation, flood visualization, and overall usability during simulated flood scenarios.

**Local DRRM Personnel.** Five (5) to ten (10) members of local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management (DRRM) units or barangay officials responsible for flood monitoring and response within the Pasig pilot area. These participants will evaluate the administrative dashboard, particularly the flood verification and moderation workflow, and provide expert feedback on the system's practical utility for disaster response coordination.

**IT and Subject-Matter Experts.** Three (3) to five (5) information technology professionals or academic faculty with expertise in geographic information systems, machine learning, or disaster informatics. These participants will assess the technical architecture, NLP accuracy, and routing performance of the system through structured evaluation criteria.

This study will use **purposive sampling** to select all participants. This method was chosen because the study requires respondents who have specific knowledge, experience, or direct involvement in flood-affected urban mobility within Pasig City, rather than a random cross-section of the general population.

For the pilot testing site, the research team will purposively select a localized sector within Pasig City that has an active flood risk and accessible deployment conditions, such as the streets surrounding the Pamantasan ng Lungsod ng Pasig campus. Commuters and motorists will then be purposively chosen based on their regular use of roads within this pilot area, ensuring that respondents have real, firsthand experience navigating flood-prone streets. DRRM personnel and barangay officials will be purposively selected based on their direct role in flood monitoring and disaster response within the pilot area, since their input is essential in evaluating the system's administrative and validation features. IT and subject-matter experts will be purposively chosen based on their relevant background in geographic information systems, machine learning, or disaster informatics, ensuring that the technical evaluation of the system is conducted by individuals with the appropriate expertise.

By using purposive sampling across all three respondent groups, the study ensures that feedback comes from individuals who are directly relevant to the system's development and evaluation. This approach allows for a more accurate and meaningful assessment of the system's usability, practical value, and technical reliability before broader implementation.

## **2.4 Data Collection Methods** {#2.4-data-collection-methods}

This study will use a quantitative approach to evaluate the LANES (Localised Alternative Navigation for Environs under Submersion) system, consistent with the Developmental Research Design presented in Section 2.2. Data collection will focus on measurable, numerical indicators of the system's technical performance and user acceptance, gathered through structured testing and standardized questionnaires. Data collection will take place in three main phases: technical baseline testing, user simulation testing, and stakeholder evaluation.

Before any data collection, informed consent will be obtained from all participants, including commuters, DRRM personnel, and IT experts. The consent form will be written in plain Filipino and English to ensure that all participants understand the purpose of the study, what their participation involves, and their right to withdraw at any time without consequence. Consent will be collected through a **Google Forms** questionnaire, along with the rest of the study's data gathering instruments.

Once consent is secured, the technical baseline testing phase will begin. In this phase, the research team will compile a curated test dataset of 300 to 500 simulated and real-world flood-related text samples (e.g., news headlines, social media posts, and citizen reports written in English and Taglish). Each text sample will have a manually annotated ground truth indicating the correct location entity and flood severity level. This annotated dataset serves as the benchmark for measuring the NLP parser's precision, recall, and F1-score before any user interaction occurs.

The user simulation testing phase will involve commuters and motorist participants interacting with the LANES application in a controlled testing environment. Participants will be given a set of navigation tasks, such as requesting a route from a specified origin to a destination while the system simulates active flood zones along the path. During these sessions, the system will automatically record quantitative interaction data, including route calculation success rates, rerouting response times, and task completion times.

After completing the navigation tasks, participants will answer a **System Usability Scale (SUS) questionnaire**, distributed through Google Forms and adapted for the local context. This questionnaire will rate the application's ease of use, clarity of flood visualization, and confidence in the rerouting suggestions using a five-point Likert scale, with statements such as, "The app showed me a safe route away from the flood," and "The flood markers on the map were easy to understand." Responses will be scored numerically to produce an overall usability score.

DRRM personnel will evaluate the administrative dashboard by simulating the moderation workflow, reviewing AI-detected flood reports, and using the approve-or-reject interface. Their evaluation will be collected through a structured, closed-ended Google Forms questionnaire based on the ISO/IEC 25010 quality characteristics discussed in Section 2.2, rating the dashboard's accuracy, efficiency, and reliability using a Likert scale.

IT and subject-matter experts will conduct a technical evaluation of the system's components, including the NLP extraction pipeline, the geospatial database setup, and the routing engine integration. Their assessment will also be collected through a structured Google Forms questionnaire using Likert-scale items covering NLP precision and recall, routing engine correctness, system response time, and overall architectural soundness.

Before conducting the main data collection, a pilot test will be performed with a small group of three to five users to ensure that the navigation tasks, Google Forms questionnaires, and system configuration are clear, functional, and appropriately scoped. This step helps refine and validate the data collection instruments before they are administered to the full sample.

## **2.5 Data Analysis Procedures** {#2.5-data-analysis-procedures}

This study will use several quantitative and qualitative analytical methods to examine the data collected from system performance logs, user testing sessions, and expert evaluations. These procedures aim to convert raw data into meaningful findings that directly address the research objectives related to NLP extraction accuracy, routing effectiveness, system usability, and practical value for flood navigation.

To evaluate the NLP parser's performance, the study will compare the system's extracted entities against the manually annotated ground truth dataset using standard information extraction metrics. Precision will measure the proportion of correctly extracted location entities and severity keywords out of all extractions made by the system. Recall will measure the proportion of correctly extracted entities out of all entities present in the test texts. The F1-score, the harmonic mean of precision and recall, will serve as the primary composite accuracy metric. These metrics will be computed overall and broken down by severity tier and by language (English vs. Taglish) to identify any performance variations. A precision and recall target of 80% or higher will be considered acceptable, reflecting established benchmarks in crisis NLP literature.

The routing engine's performance will be evaluated through task completion analysis. For each user navigation task, the study will record whether the system successfully generated a route that avoided all active flood zones. The overall success rate will be calculated as the percentage of tasks where the route completely avoided orange and red severity polygons. In addition, average rerouting response time will be measured from the moment a user submits a route request to the moment the alternative path is displayed. Task completion rates and response times will be summarized using mean, standard deviation, and percentage distributions.

System usability will be assessed through the System Usability Scale (SUS) scores collected from commuter and motorist participants. The SUS yields a single composite score ranging from 0 to 100, which will be interpreted using established adjective rating benchmarks (e.g., above 68 considered above average, above 80 considered excellent). Scores will be summarized using mean and standard deviation, and individual item responses will be analyzed using frequencies to identify specific usability strengths and weaknesses, such as clarity of flood markers and confidence in rerouting suggestions.

Feedback from DRRM personnel and IT experts will be analyzed using a combination of descriptive statistics and thematic analysis. Scored items from technical evaluation checklists and dashboards will be summarized using mean ratings and percentage distributions. Open-ended responses from semi-structured interviews will be analyzed qualitatively using thematic coding to identify recurring patterns, concerns, and recommendations regarding the system's practical deployment potential. All statistical analyses will be carried out using established data analysis tools such as Python's data science libraries (NumPy, Pandas, SciPy) for quantitative metrics and standard qualitative coding software or manual thematic analysis for interview data. By combining objective performance metrics with subjective user feedback, the study will produce a well-rounded evaluation of the LANES system's technical robustness and real-world applicability.

## **2.6 Ethical Considerations** {#2.6-ethical-considerations}

This study places strong emphasis on ethical standards, particularly because it involves collecting location-based data and user interaction data in a disaster-response context where participant safety and data privacy are paramount. All procedures are designed to ensure the safety, privacy, comfort, and well-being of every participant throughout the entire study. The ethical considerations focus on informed consent, data privacy, participant safety, responsible use of AI, transparency in flood information, and institutional approval.

Before any data is collected, the researchers will secure written informed consent from all participants, including commuters, motorists, DRRM personnel, and IT experts. The consent form will be written in plain language in both English and Filipino, clearly explaining the purpose of the study, what participation involves, how data will be used, and the participant's right to withdraw at any time without penalty. Participants will be explicitly informed that their navigation task data, survey responses, and feedback will be used solely for research and system improvement purposes.

Data privacy and confidentiality form another essential part of the study's ethics. All location data, route histories, survey responses, and interview recordings will be anonymized immediately upon collection. No personally identifiable information, such as participant names, home addresses, or exact daily commuting patterns, will be included in any stored dataset. Location data collected during testing sessions will be stored separately from participant identifiers and will be used only for aggregate performance analysis. All data will be encrypted and stored on password-secured storage accessible only to the core research team. These data will be used solely for research purposes and will be permanently deleted at the end of the study.
