Compliance with the Data Privacy Act of 2012 and related guidelines will be strictly observed.

To protect the well-being of participants, the study is designed to involve minimal risk. The simulation testing will be conducted in a controlled environment, not in actual flood conditions, ensuring that no participant is exposed to real danger while testing the system. Participants will not be asked to navigate through actual flooded streets, and all testing scenarios will use simulated flood data. Any observation or interview sessions will be conducted in a safe, comfortable, and familiar setting within the pilot testing site.

Responsible use of AI is also an important ethical concern. The NLP model used in LANES will process only publicly available text data from news feeds and simulated inputs during testing. No personal communications or private social media accounts will be scraped. The system's administrative moderation workflow ensures that no AI-detected flood report is published to the public map without human verification, providing a safeguard against false positives that could mislead commuters. The flood severity classification will be clearly labeled with its AI-derived confidence level, and the system will include a disclaimer that all flood markers are AI-generated estimates subject to moderation and should be used alongside official warnings from PAGASA and local DRRM offices.

Finally, before implementation, the complete research protocol—including data collection instruments, consent forms, testing scripts, and system evaluation procedures—will be submitted to the appropriate Institutional Ethics Review Committee. This ensures compliance with national and international standards for research involving human participants and reinforces the commitment to conducting ethical, responsible, and transparent research.

## **2.7 Summary** {#2.7-summary}

The selected software driven, three tier architecture and Agile development methodology are uniquely suited to satisfy the research objectives of the LANES platform without requiring expensive physical hardware installations. By partitioning the system logic into distinct, specialized layers the Data Ingestion Layer, Linguistic Processing Engine, Spatial Persistence Layer, Routing Topology Interface, and Cross-Platform Presentation Layer the architecture provides a robust structure capable of transforming volatile, conversational Taglish text reports into live, flood-adaptive navigation paths.

The integration of a bilingual Named Entity Recognition (NER) model addresses the primary objective of extracting meaningful street-level location and severity data from unstructured public streams, while the utilization of information retrieval metrics ensures the system's linguistic parsing capabilities remain mathematically verifiable. Downstream, the application of PostGIS geometric buffering algorithms satisfies the objective of turning those linguistic variables into real-world geographic blockades by automatically scaling single coordinates into 50-meter avoidance polygons. Finally, intercepting the routing cost matrix within the Open Source Routing Machine (OSRM) to update affected road edge weights to infinity ensures that pathfinding calculations dynamically deflect commuter route arrays away from active flood zones in real time.

Executing this technical architecture under an Agile framework ensures that these high-dependency modules are progressively developed, integrated, and evaluated in structured sprint cycles. This iterative management strategy, combined with isolated machine learning testing libraries and automated routing validation scripts, guarantees that the system achieves high operational accuracy and system reliability. Ultimately, this methodology establishes a cohesive, entirely code-based framework that successfully demonstrates the viability of leveraging natural language processing and spatial analytics to deliver responsive, data-driven municipal disaster routing solutions for Pasig City.
